main_ingredients: chicken thigh or breast (pan-fried or grilled)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of chicken chop spaghetti as served in a Singaporean Western food stall or café typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a pan-fried or grilled chicken piece (~150 g) and a portion of spaghetti with tomato sauce (~200 g).

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Chop Spaghetti — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Chop Spaghetti (cr_id F05060026). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 (data not available in source).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database — spaghetti with meat sauce analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken chop spaghetti as a named composite dish. GI of 49 (low) is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for spaghetti with meat sauce (GI ~45–52, mean ~49), which is the closest defensible analogous food — a pasta dish with protein-rich meat component and tomato-based sauce. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (49) × available carbohydrate per serving (350 g × 9.32 g/100g = 32.6 g) ÷ 100 = 16.0, rounded to 16.1. Input record GI confidence was 0.614, consistent with an analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 328 mg per 100 g; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,148 mg sodium, representing 50% of the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced seasoning and low-sodium sauce when ordering, and limit portion size to no more than 200 g to keep single-meal sodium below 660 mg; pairing with a potassium-rich side such as steamed vegetables can partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI of 49 and moderate carbohydrate content (9.32 g per 100 g) are favourable, but a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 32.6 g available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~16, which is in the medium GL range.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include this dish in a controlled meal plan by limiting the serving to 250–300 g, requesting wholegrain spaghetti to increase dietary fibre and further lower GI, and avoiding cream-based or high-sugar sauces in favour of the tomato-based preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 328 mg per 100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern; total fat (4.75 g per 100 g) is moderate, and saturated fat data are not available from the HPB source but are estimated to be present from the chicken skin and cooking oil.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, choose a grilled skinless chicken preparation over pan-fried with skin, request tomato-based sauce without added cream or butter, limit portion to 250 g, and pair with a fibre-rich salad to support LDL reduction and blood pressure control.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomato-based sauce contains nightshade solanine alkaloids that may exacerbate joint inflammation in solanine-sensitive RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients with known nightshade sensitivity should request a mushroom or olive oil-based sauce instead of tomato sauce; olive oil and garlic in the preparation provide anti-inflammatory oleocanthal and allicin, which are beneficial if the nightshade component is avoided.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Chop Spaghetti is a popular Western-influenced Singaporean hawker and café dish consisting of a pan-fried or grilled chicken piece — typically a bone-in or boneless thigh or breast — served alongside a portion of spaghetti tossed in tomato-based sauce, accompanied by brown gravy poured over the chicken. The dish reflects Singapore's Eurasian and colonial culinary heritage, where Western cooking techniques and pasta were adapted to local tastes and hawker-stall economics. The chicken is typically marinated in soy sauce, garlic, and pepper before frying, giving it a savoury, slightly caramelised exterior with a juicy interior. The spaghetti is cooked al dente and coated in a mildly spiced tomato sauce. Per 100 g, the dish provides 116 kcal, 8.93 g protein, 4.75 g fat, 9.32 g carbohydrate, 2.55 g sugar, 0.88 g dietary fibre, and 328 mg sodium. The protein content is substantial for a pasta dish due to the chicken component. Sodium is the primary nutritional concern at 328 mg per 100 g, making a full plate (approximately 350 g) deliver around 1,148 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 49 (low), making it relatively favourable for blood glucose management compared to plain pasta, though the glycemic load per serving is in the medium range (~16). Clinically, the dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in controlled portions with wholegrain pasta substitution, requires sodium moderation for hypertension and cardiovascular disease, and warrants caution for rheumatoid arthritis patients sensitive to nightshade vegetables due to the tomato sauce component. It is commonly served as a working lunch or casual dinner in Singaporean Western food stalls, coffee shops, and cafés.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060026) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (spaghetti with meat sauce, GI ~49), which is the closest defensible approved GI source; this introduces a minor uncertainty but does not reduce overall confidence below high given the strong macro provenance.
notes: 1. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (F05060026) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite Singaporean dish as of 2026. 2. GI value of 49 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for spaghetti with meat sauce; no direct GI measurement for chicken chop spaghetti exists in any approved GI source. 3. Serving size of 350 g is an operational estimate for a standard Singaporean hawker-stall plate; no standardised portion weight is published in the HPB source for this dish. 4. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 term exists for chicken chop spaghetti as a named Singaporean composite dish. 5. Halal dietary flag is set based on cultural context (Eurasian/Western Singaporean hawker dish commonly prepared halal); individual vendor certification should be verified. 6. All core macros (protein_g 8.93, fat_g 4.75, carbohydrate_g 9.32) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
